the inequality involves the sum of fractional parts of powers of $ x $, starting from $ x $, $ x^3 $, $ x^5 $, ..., up to $ x^{99} $. the fractional part of a number $ y $, denoted $ \{y\} $, is defined as $ y - \lfloor y \rfloor $, where $ \lfloor y \rfloor $ is the greatest integer less than or equal to $ y $. this means that $ \{y\} $ is always between 0 (inclusive) and 1 (exclusive). 

the inequality is asking if there exists a real number $ x > 1 $ that is not an integer and satisfies the condition that the sum of these fractional parts is less than $ \frac{1}{2^{99}} $. this is a very stringent condition, as the sum of fractional parts is expected to be very small. let's analyze the structure of the inequality.

first, note that for any real number $ x $, the fractional part $ \{x\} $ is always between 0 and 1. since $ x > 1 $, the powers $ x^3, x^5, \ldots, x^{99} $ will also be greater than 1, and their fractional parts will be between 0 and 1. the sum of these fractional parts is the sum of 49 terms, each between 0 and 1. the sum of 49 terms, each less than 1, will be less than 49. but the inequality requires this sum to be less than $ \frac{1}{2^{99}} $, which is an extremely small number.
